title: Entangling gates for the SU(N) anyons
abstract: The model of a topological quantum computer is a promising one due to its natural resistance to noise and other errors. Operations in such a computer are implemented by braiding the trajectories of anyons. While it is easy to understand how to build one-qubit operations, two-qubit operations are more difficult. In arXiv:2412.20931 we suggested an approach to build such operations for a topological quantum computer based on SU(2) Chern-Simons theory with arbitrary level using cabling of knots. In this paper we discuss how this approach should be generalized to the SU(N) case, what the differences are, and which new problems arise.
summary: This paper extends the framework for topological quantum computing from SU(2) to SU(N) anyons. It proposes a cabling method to construct complex entangling gates from simpler ones and provides numerical evidence for high-fidelity braid patterns that minimize leakage into non-computational states.
main_problem: Generalizing the construction of entangling two-qubit gates from SU(2) anyons to the more complex SU(N) case while addressing the leakage problem into higher-dimensional representations.
main_result: The authors successfully identified specific braid sequences for SU(N) (N=3 to 6) that achieve high-fidelity entangling gates using co-directional and counter-directional cabling techniques.
method: The study uses the Reshetikhin-Turaev approach, quantum group theory (Uq(su(N))), and a cabling technique to relate higher-representation matrices to fundamental R-matrices, supplemented by numerical brute-force searches for braid patterns.
model_or_system: A topological quantum computer model based on 3D SU(N) Chern-Simons gauge theory, where quantum operations are implemented via the braiding of anyonic trajectories.
key_observables: Process probability (fidelity), phase deviation (delta phi), and the entanglement content of the effective operation matrix.
important_parameters: The rank of the group (N), the Chern-Simons level (k), the quantum parameter (q), and the braid sequence weights.
main_assumptions: Anyon trajectories can be controlled topologically without intersection; the root of unity q is sufficiently large to avoid representation theory complications; and the use of vertical framing for consistency.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate R-matrix crossings, braid/plat representations of knots (trefoil), the cabling process for strands, and visual representations of the identified high-precision braid sequences.
paper_structure: The paper begins by motivating the need for SU(N) gates, establishes the mathematical framework of Chern-Simons theory and quantum groups, details the cabling method for constructing higher-order gates, addresses the leakage and framing problems, and concludes with numerical results for specific SU(N) configurations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
